reason: The item mixes two separable judgment axes—barrier layer composition/arrangement effects (A/C/F) and confinement vs open-space severity (G)—which weakens cognitive coherence and increases boundary ambiguity about what evidence set a solver should privilege. Decoys D and E both hinge on unsupported mechanistic attribution (radiation-dominance/sole-cause), reducing single-fault diversity and discriminative signal. Revising to either (i) split into two items or (ii) tighten the stem to explicitly scope the inference domain (configuration comparisons only vs environmental condition effects) would improve diagnostic value.

The item has a coherent central theme (TRP timing + staged heat-transfer suppression), but several options invite mechanistic interpretation beyond what is explicitly pinned down in the stem, making the epistemic boundary slightly gray. Decoys are mostly plausible yet two rely on closely related overreach patterns (unsupported generalization/conditioning), which weakens single-fault purity and reduces discriminative separation for careful experts.

The prompt tightly anchors the evaluation to a specific boundary mistake: extrapolating a no-agent heat-transfer threshold and conflating flame suppression with propagation prevention, while the scenario explicitly states propagation persists post-suppression. It elicits a clear engineering critique plus a required additional measure (auxiliary cooling) without inviting broad speculation about agent classes. The main quality risk is that it is short-answer without options, so decoy-based discriminative control is weaker and some respondents may give partially correct critiques that are harder to score consistently.
